---
title: "Partridge Pea (Chamaecrista fasciculata)"
slug: "chamaecrista-fasciculata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/356684561/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Partridge Pea"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Fabales"
tax_family: "Fabaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Fabales", "Fabaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/356684561/medium.jpeg" alt="Partridge Pea" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/lmcconachie">
lmcconachie</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/202003843">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Fabales
-   **Family:** Fabaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=127907)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in open, sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

An annual wildflower characterized by its pinnate leaves and bright yellow flowers with reddish markings. In Florida, it is commonly found in open, sandy habitats such as fields, roadsides, and prairies. It grows during the warmer months and produces seed pods that are an important food source for wildlife, while also attracting a variety of pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](http://inaturalist.org/taxa/127907-Chamaecrista-fasciculata)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-chamaecrista-fasciculata/)
